narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors, estimated a 43.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This probability, while not overwhelmingly high, crossed our threshold for admission decisions, as the model aims to select students with the strongest projected outcomes. For context, you're a Black male with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.90—well above the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 40, also stronger than the average of 36.70, alongside coming from a lower-middle family income bracket and planning full-time studies. The model's decision hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, with your LSAT score emerging as the single most influential one, as it strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar. Next in importance was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—below the average of 5.53—which pulled the prediction in the opposite direction, toward a higher risk of failure by signaling relatively weaker performance in that stage compared to peers. Your undergraduate GPA ranked third in influence and also worked in your favor, reinforcing the model's lean toward bar passage due to its strength relative to most applicants. Overall, while your standout test scores and college grades helped balance things out, the dip in your later high school deciles tipped the scales enough to raise concerns about bar readiness. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—many from similar backgrounds succeed with targeted preparation—but rather a data-driven flag on how your grades trended. Compared to typical students who pass the bar on the first try, your profile shows brighter spots in standardized testing and undergrad work but lags in school rankings, which the model weighs heavily for law school success patterns. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through community college or targeted tutoring, and reapply after building that record. You might also explore bar prep resources early or schools with holistic reviews that value your LSAT and GPA strengths more flexibly. With focus on those weaker areas, your profile has real promise for a stronger prediction next time. Keep pushing—law needs voices like yours.
